metadata.selection_goal: Identify non-memory-corruption CVEs from the libc6/glibc corpus whose root causes could survive a Rust rewrite.
selection_scope: Core libc APIs and runtime behavior.
NSS, resolver, regex, glob, wordexp, iconv, crypt, and related library-side helpers.
Library-side nscd client behavior when it is part of libc call paths.
selection_rules: Included semantic, parser, state-machine, privilege-boundary, entropy, concurrency, hardening, ABI, and resource-management bugs.

Excluded primary memory-corruption and memory-safety CVEs such as buffer overflows, out-of-bounds access, use-after-free, double free, heap corruption, and similar bugs that Rust safe code directly targets.

Excluded CVEs limited to helper tools or daemons outside the likely libc6 Rust port, such as the nscd daemon, pt_chown, locale/ldd/catchsegv, and the standalone iconv utility, unless the issue touched library-side runtime code.
Retained a small number of disputed or release-not-affected CVEs when they still describe a bug class that a Rust reimplementation could plausibly reproduce; such caveats are recorded per entry.
excluded_examples: nscd daemon-only crashes and memory-corruption bugs
buffer-overflow / out-of-bounds / use-after-free issues in iconv, malloc, syslog, resolver, printf, glob, and loader code
tool-only issues in catchsegv, locale, ldd, pt_chown, and the standalone iconv command
metadata.ordering_rule: Entries are sorted by published date descending from all_cves.json; CVE ID year is not used because some older bugs received CVE publication dates later.

relevant_cves.description: iconv() aborts on IBM1390/IBM1399 conversion because malformed input reaches an internal assertion.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port still needs total state-machine handling for malformed multibyte input and should not rely on assertions for externally reachable cases.

relevant_cves.description: gethostbyaddr[_r] can return an invalid DNS hostname that violates the DNS specification.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust will not fix semantic DNS validation mistakes; reverse lookup code still has to validate labels and returned names.

relevant_cves.description: A crafted reverse-DNS response can make gethostbyaddr[_r] treat a non-answer section as a valid answer.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Resolver section parsing and answer selection are language-agnostic correctness and security rules.

relevant_cves.description: The libc-side nscd client can compare concurrently modified shared data and crash under load.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust porting does not remove cross-process shared-memory races; cache-client synchronization and snapshotting still need explicit design.
caveats: Touches library-side nscd client code, not the nscd daemon.
Current Ubuntu releases listed in the corpus mark shipped versions as not affected.

relevant_cves.description: Fork-concurrent calls can return predictable randomness because post-fork RNG state is not refreshed correctly.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Fork safety and RNG reseeding semantics remain relevant in Rust, especially around libc-compatible RNG APIs.
caveats: Ubuntu tracker notes released upstream and Ubuntu versions were not affected; retained as a design pitfall.

relevant_cves.component: ld.so / static setuid dlopen path handling
relevant_cves.description: Statically linked setuid binaries that use dlopen can honor attacker-controlled LD_LIBRARY_PATH.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Secure-exec environment scrubbing is a policy issue, not a memory-safety issue; a Rust loader/runtime can repeat it exactly.

relevant_cves.description: A powerpc getrandom implementation returns EINVAL as the function result instead of setting errno.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: ABI and errno contract bugs survive language changes; a Rust port still has to match libc calling conventions exactly.
caveats: Ubuntu tracker marks shipped versions as not affected.

relevant_cves.description: A crafted **(!() pattern can crash fnmatch().
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Pattern-parser termination and error recovery are parser-logic problems, not pointer-safety problems.

relevant_cves.description: SHA-crypt password hashing has quadratic runtime in password length, enabling CPU-exhaustion attacks.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: If a Rust port preserves SHA-crypt compatibility, it inherits the same algorithmic denial-of-service characteristic.
caveats: Inherent to the legacy algorithm more than to a specific C implementation.

relevant_cves.description: realpath() can return an unexpected value and disclose path information.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Path canonicalization semantics are easy to get subtly wrong even in safe code.

relevant_cves.description: Invalid multibyte sequences in several IBM encodings can leave iconv() without forward progress.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Termination guarantees in decoder state machines are independent of Rust memory safety.

relevant_cves.description: Invalid ISO-2022-JP-3 input can trip an assertion and abort iconv().
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: A Rust port must handle malformed encoding transitions as recoverable errors, not as internal invariants.

relevant_cves.description: Converting UCS4 text with an irreversible character can trip an assertion and abort iconv().
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Decoder invariants around irreversible conversions still need explicit error handling in Rust.

relevant_cves.description: Secure execution on x86-64 failed to ignore LD_PREFER_MAP_32BIT_EXEC after a security transition.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Secure-mode environment handling and address-space policy are loader logic bugs, not C-only bugs.

relevant_cves.description: A crafted regex can trigger uncontrolled recursion in regexec().
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Recursive matcher logic can still overflow stack or hang in Rust if structurally similar.
caveats: Upstream and Ubuntu did not consider exposure to untrusted regex patterns a default security boundary.

relevant_cves.non_memory_type: parser correctness / assertion denial of service
relevant_cves.description: regcomp() misparses alternatives, causing incorrect results or an assertion failure.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Formal parser correctness and total handling of malformed regexes remain necessary in Rust.

relevant_cves.description: x32 memcmp can incorrectly report equality because register handling is wrong.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Even in Rust, architecture-specific intrinsics or asm paths must preserve exact comparison semantics.

relevant_cves.description: getaddrinfo() can accept an IPv4 string followed by whitespace and arbitrary trailing data.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust will not prevent permissive or ambiguous parsers from being used in security-sensitive contexts.

relevant_cves.description: Resolving a crafted hostname can allocate a socket descriptor that is never closed.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Resource-lifetime bugs remain relevant in Rust at FFI and OS boundary layers.

relevant_cves.description: An empty RPATH/RUNPATH token inside privileged loading can be misread as the current directory.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Search-path normalization rules are pure logic; a Rust loader can reproduce the same privilege bug.

relevant_cves.description: EDNS support solicits large UDP responses, making off-path DNS spoofing easier via fragmentation.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Resolver hardening choices persist across languages; compatibility code must still respect anti-spoofing design constraints.

relevant_cves.description: Extended regular-expression processing can hit an assertion in pop_fail_stack and crash.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust regex-compatible code can still encode invalid internal states or rely on assertions for hostile input.

relevant_cves.description: ARM EABI makecontext creates contexts that are incompatible with unwinding, leading to hangs.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Coroutine and context-switch ABI rules are independent of memory safety; Rust code still has to obey unwinder and calling-convention contracts.

relevant_cves.description: Out-of-range time values can crash strftime() or leak information.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Boundary handling and normalization of time structures remain correctness bugs in Rust.

relevant_cves.description: LD_POINTER_GUARD can disable pointer-guarding across privilege boundaries.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Security-hardening policy enforcement in secure-exec mode is language-agnostic.

relevant_cves.description: Looking up a database while iterating it can reset the file pointer and loop forever.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Shared iterator and state management in NSS backends is still a logic problem in Rust.

relevant_cves.description: A positive DNS answer during network-name processing can send getnetbyname into an infinite loop.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Forward-progress guarantees in parsers and resolvers do not come from Rust automatically.

relevant_cves.description: wordexp() fails to enforce WRDE_NOCMD and can execute commands anyway.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: API flag semantics and shell-expansion policy must be preserved explicitly in any reimplementation.

relevant_cves.description: Locale-related environment variables can use .. to traverse directories and bypass restrictions.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Environment-variable sanitization and path canonicalization remain key loader and runtime pitfalls in Rust.

relevant_cves.description: Statically linked executables could use an all-zero pointer guard because the random guard was never initialized.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Hardening-state initialization is independent of memory safety; secure-startup logic must still be correct.

relevant_cves.description: Large numbers of RPC connections can drive svc_run() into excessive CPU use.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Event-loop fairness and denial-of-service resistance are still relevant if legacy SunRPC APIs are reimplemented.

relevant_cves.description: addmntent() can silently ignore write failures, making /etc/mtab corruption easier.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Correct errno and status propagation is a compatibility and security requirement for libc wrappers in any language.

relevant_cves.description: $ORIGIN-only RPATH values can be expanded in privileged programs in unsafe ways.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Privileged token-expansion rules are loader policy, not memory-safety.

relevant_cves.description: Crafted unmatched glob expressions can consume excessive CPU and memory.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Rust ports still need resource limits or linear-time behavior for hostile glob patterns.
caveats: Upstream and Ubuntu treated this more as an application-input hardening issue.

relevant_cves.description: Adjacent repetition operators can cause unbounded stack consumption in regcomp().
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Recursive parser or automaton construction can still exhaust stack in Rust if translated directly.
caveats: Upstream and Ubuntu treated this as debatable security significance.

relevant_cves.description: Adjacent bounded repetitions can bypass RE_DUP_MAX handling and crash regcomp().
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Enforcing numeric parser and engine limits is language-agnostic.
caveats: Upstream and Ubuntu treated this as debatable security significance.

relevant_cves.description: LD_AUDIT was not sufficiently restricted to true audit DSOs, enabling privilege escalation via unsafe libraries in trusted directories.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Secure loader policy around audit objects remains relevant in any reimplementation.

relevant_cves.description: $ORIGIN in LD_AUDIT can load attacker-controlled DSOs from arbitrary directories.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Secure-mode expansion of loader tokens is a logic bug a Rust loader can still reproduce.

relevant_cves.description: Newlines in mountpoint names are encoded incorrectly, allowing mtab corruption or mount-option injection.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Serialization and escaping rules for privileged text formats remain error-prone in Rust.

relevant_cves.description: passwd.adjunct.byname data can be merged into passwd results, exposing encrypted NIS passwords.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust: Backend composition and data-redaction policy are semantic issues, not memory-safety issues.
